person.name: Zach Sykes
person.summary: Full-stack software engineer with 5+ years of experience building scalable web platforms and developer-first systems. Proven expertise in React, TypeScript, and backend patterns. Experienced shipping 0-to-1 products, building reusable component libraries, and designing APIs that scale across teams. Strong track record of collaborating with product and design partners to deliver user-focused features in fast-moving environments.
person.timeZone: Mountain Time (Denver, CO) — flexible across time zones
person.lookingFor: A role that challenges me, working for a company whose work I can find meaning in, where I get to ship cool things and help architect new ideas. Remote-first teams with strong async communication and trust. Curious about teams that invest in internal tooling and maintainable systems.

narrative.originStory: I wanted new challenges, and the work-life balance of kitchen work had run its course. I knew I could do something more mentally demanding — and engineering turned out to be the perfect mix of creativity and structure for me.
narrative.bridge: I quit my kitchen job, sold my car to float the income gap, and went full-time through General Assembly's 3-month software engineering bootcamp. Outside of class I logged countless hours on YouTube tutorials, documentation, and side experiments — that self-directed time is what actually made the transition stick.
narrative.carryover: Kitchen leadership is constant quick decision-making (shipping fast and efficiently), tightening systems for reliability, coaching the people around you, and forecasting problems before they happen. That's most of what senior engineering work actually is — different artifacts, same instincts.

roles.title: Full Stack Software Engineer
roles.summary: Build and maintain production web applications and APIs serving enterprise users — owning architecture, delivery, reliability, and operational health across the full stack.
achievements: Lead end-to-end feature delivery using React, TypeScript, Next.js, and .NET — from UI design through backend API implementation, cloud deployment, and production monitoring, with full ownership.

Built a real-time collaborative platform (Planning Poker) as a reusable tool on top of internal infrastructure — independently architected and shipped from 0 to 1, including WebSocket infrastructure and backend services.
Designed and established React/TypeScript component systems and API integration patterns adopted across multiple teams — improved consistency and developer velocity.

Built and maintained CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, Azure Pipelines) and production observability tooling (Grafana, Application Insights) — enabling safe, rapid releases and active incident response.

Collaborated directly with product, design, and business stakeholders to translate requirements into shipped features — skilled at communicating technical tradeoffs and architectural decisions across disciplines.
Mentored junior engineers on code quality, architecture patterns, and best practices — actively invested in raising team-wide technical standards.
Integrate AI coding tools (GitHub Copilot, Claude) into daily engineering workflows — measurably improving delivery speed and enabling deeper focus on architecture and problem-solving.

roles.title: Founder / Freelance Engineer
roles.summary: Freelance practice building production websites and e-commerce work for small-business clients from requirements through deployment. Own UI/UX through implementation; calathea.design itself is a shipped Next.js site showcasing that standard.
achievements: Shipped 5 client sites end-to-end since 2024 — ranging across e-commerce storefronts, portfolios for musicians and artists, movie promotion sites, and WordPress sites for nonprofits.
Own every layer of each engagement — UI/UX design, frontend implementation, backend integrations, third-party APIs (Stripe where applicable), and cloud deployment on Vercel.
Built and launched the business site itself (calathea.design) as a production Next.js application — same quality standard delivered to clients.
Direct client communication from discovery through deployment and post-launch iteration — no intermediaries between requirements and shipped work.

roles.title: Kitchen Manager
roles.summary: Progressed from line cook to Kitchen Manager. Led daily operations, team management, and process improvement in a high-volume environment.
achievements: Led and managed a 40+ person team — hiring, onboarding, training, scheduling, performance management, and culture building.

projects.name: Planning Poker
projects.summary: Real-time estimation platform built for distributed delivery teams at Forvis Mazars. Replaced manual estimation coordination with a single internal workflow that improved session consistency and reduced meeting friction.
outcomes: Shipped from 0 to 1 as reusable internal tooling, including WebSocket infrastructure and backend services.
Adopted across Forvis Mazars software teams for real-time distributed estimation sessions.

projects.name: zach.dev portfolio assistant
projects.summary: Recruiter-friendly portfolio chat over a structured resume: Next.js chat UI, ASP.NET Core streaming backend, Anthropic integration, request validation, and API abuse safeguards. Built to demonstrate building around AI for an actual purpose — not just experimenting with it.
outcomes: Working example of agentic chat over structured data with a real backend, validation, and safeguards.
Source code public for review at github.com/Calathea-Z/portfolio_ai.

projects.name: Portfolio MCP resume server
projects.summary: stdio Model Context Protocol server that registers the same seven structured resume tools as the portfolio chat (get_role, search_resume, list_projects_by_skill, get_metrics, list_recent_shipped, get_narrative, get_faq). Loads the canonical resume.json via --data for Claude Desktop and other MCP clients. JSON input schemas under mcp/schemas are parity-tested in .NET against ResumeToolInputSchemas so Anthropic and MCP surfaces stay aligned.
outcomes: Same tool contracts as the streaming chat API, exposed at the protocol layer instead of a bespoke HTTP integration.
Public write-up and demo video at https://zachsykes.dev/projects/mcp-server; implementation in the portfolio repo under mcp/.

projects.name: Calathea Web Design client sites
projects.summary: Marketing and e-commerce sites for small-business clients end-to-end: architecture, implementation, deployment, and iteration with direct client feedback.
outcomes: Range: e-commerce storefronts, musician/artist portfolios, movie promotion sites, nonprofit WordPress sites.
Full lifecycle ownership per engagement — discovery through post-launch iteration.

metrics.note: Every software team across all six verticals adopted the tool after launch.

metrics.label: Client sites shipped end-to-end via Calathea
metrics.note: Since 2024 — mix of e-commerce, portfolios, promotional sites, and nonprofit WordPress.

education.note: Full-time, 3-month immersive program. Career change from operations leadership into software engineering, supplemented by extensive self-directed study via documentation, tutorials, and side projects.

beliefs: Reliability beats novelty in production systems. New tools are welcome, but only when they reduce risk or improve developer throughput measurably.
Clear contracts matter: typed boundaries, explicit API behavior, and predictable ownership between frontend, backend, and data layers.
Observability is part of feature delivery, not a follow-up task.
Mentoring and code-review quality compound over time and are part of senior engineering impact, not side work.

faq.question: What kind of role are you looking for next?
faq.answer: A role that challenges me, working for a company whose work I can find meaning in, where I get to ship cool things and help architect new ideas. Full-time is the primary path; Calathea-style freelance is also on the table for the right engagement.

faq.question: Why did you build this chatbot?
faq.answer: To demonstrate I know how to build around and with AI for an actual purpose — not just experiment with it. This site is a working example: agentic chat over structured resume data, with a real ASP.NET Core streaming backend, request validation, and safeguards against abuse. Source is public at github.com/Calathea-Z/portfolio_ai.

faq.question: What is the MCP server on your portfolio?
faq.answer: A Node.js stdio server using the Model Context Protocol SDK. It exposes the exact same seven resume-backed tools as this chat so Claude Desktop (or any MCP host) can call them against the same resume.json file—no second HTTP API to maintain. Input schemas live in mcp/schemas and are checked in CI against the C# ResumeToolInputSchemas constants so the wire contract cannot drift quietly. Install notes are in the repo mcp/README.md; a short demo and copy live at https://zachsykes.dev/projects/mcp-server.

faq.question: What's your biggest engineering accomplishment so far?
faq.answer: Two things, both at Forvis Mazars. First, shipping Planning Poker from zero to a tool now used by every software team across all six verticals. Second, helping lead the migration from a Blazor frontend to React/TypeScript — including the architecture work, the rollout, and the teaching that made it stick across teams.

faq.question: How do you approach learning new tech?
faq.answer: I play with it, build small things, watch tutorials, ask questions, and fail at it until I succeed. Docs and reading have a place, but nothing teaches like shipping something half-broken and fixing it.

faq.question: Why did you switch from kitchen leadership to engineering?
faq.answer: I wanted new challenges, and the work-life balance of kitchen work had run its course. I knew I could do something more mentally demanding — engineering turned out to be the perfect mix of creativity and structure. Quit the kitchen job, sold my car to bridge the income gap, did the General Assembly bootcamp full-time, and logged countless self-directed hours alongside it.

faq.question: What's your engineering philosophy in a sentence?
faq.answer: Reliability beats novelty in production systems — new tools earn their place by reducing risk or measurably improving developer throughput.

faq.question: Tell me about a tough debug or incident.
faq.answer: I'd rather walk through a specific example in a live conversation than try to compress it here. Happy to dig into it over email or LinkedIn.
